channel.title: Prototype on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Prototype on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Seeing The Game In The Prototype
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about seeing the game in the prototype — Now.

The alternate titles for this video were going to be [snorts]- should you be a game director? Or a litmus test for game directors, because I think this is such an important ability to be able to do that. Anyone at the game director level or above should be able to do this, and if they can’t.

item.title: Toy Update
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to give you a toy update.

That — and by that I mean this — is an update on a video I did a year ago where I talked about toys I was making and those are little games and not even really don’t even want to call them games. They’re like little game ideas, mostly mechanics, things like a, 3D dice roller. Uh, turn that I started turning into to a card game dice roller thing or a procedural dungeon generator and uh, I look back at that.

item.title: The Outer Worlds Vertical Slice
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I have a very special video to show you.

It is the The Outer Worlds’s vertical slice. Before I get in, let me give you some context, and actually, before I give you context, let me thank obsidian and especially Mikey Dowling for arranging this and letting me show this to you. I want to show you the vertical slice for many reasons, and let me let me explain what a vertical slice is first, if you watched my production stages video, which I link below, I talk about the different stages that a game goes through when it’s being developed. The vertical slice stage is when you make a level of the game or a map or, in this case, an outdoor map with a few connecting Maps. I think there was a town map and three dungeon Maps.

item.title: Game Pitches
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today let’s talk about game pitches.

Yes, I look like Ernest Hemingway. A friend said I looked like Ernest Hemingway with this beard, so I put on the turtleneck and got the pipe and I’m leaning into it.

But let’s talk about game pitches for real. So wikka fish ask: could you go into more detail about pitching a game? I’m curious as to what the process may look like, as well as the materials. You should have prototypes, storyboards, Etc. In order to have a successful pitch. That is a really good question.

item.title: Fast Fail
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about a development methodology called Fast fail.

Ironically, I used to do fast fail 30 years ago, before I knew what it was called that, and I’m doing it now with my own little fun in-house games. But it hasn’t been something done recently and I want to talk about it because I really think fast fail is a good way of making games and maybe if you’re thinking about making your prototype, this may help you put it together. So what’s called Fast fail these days is something that I usually just call rapid prototyping, and if you watch the video I have on stages that um game development goes through, prototyping is one of the early stages where you quickly put together a level just to see if a mechanic works or just to see if the level size is good or the sight lines are good or whatever you’re trying to do. You do it before you have final art and final code to do rapid prototyping, you have to be in the mindset that you view failure as a good thing, that you’re not upset when something fails. You’re happy that, with as little time and resource investment as possible, you discovered something you were about to do isn’t going to work.

item.title: Game Production Stages
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game production stages.

This is actually an answer to a question I got from Charlie K 5964, who asks, as a follow-up to the ideas video, what should go into the Prototype. Should it be a minimum viable product or should it things like art be included? Now, that’s a really good question, but couple things, um, different companies call different things a prototype. So what I thought I’d do is walk through the stages that my games have gone through and I’ll tell you the names I give them.

item.title: The Six Demos Of Fallout
item.description:
Hello everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about these six demos of Fallout.

Someone left a comment, uh, last week, and asked about something about the Fallout demo and I almost said which one? Because I remember just doing a couple. Remember, this is we’re going on almost 30 years ago that Fallout started. It started in 94, and I could remember a couple, but you know I’ve taken all my notes for Fallout, all the paper notes from Fallout are now digitized. So I have 86 pages of notes and this is with not one note per page.

item.title: Arcanum Prototypes & Structures
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prototypes and structures in Arcanum.

I did the previous talk on how we did procedural generation in Arcanum and now I want to talk about how we actually stored objects. Uh, that was a question several people had and then related to the procedural generation. I mentioned that, uh, buildings were done a little differently, so I’ll explain that too. So before I can explain how prototypes work, I kind of have to explain how objects work and even why we did prototypes, which was basically to save memory.

item.title: Troika's Post Apocalyptic Demo
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim.

What you are seeing is the only known video footage of epic, which was troika’s in-house post-apocalyptic game we started designing and implementing towards the end of vampire. Uh, of course it never came out. This footage isn’t from mine.

I’ll include the link below. It’s not from my archive. I don’t have any video footage. In fact, I don’t even have any code.

I have a lot of design docs and notes, but my epic folder seems very sparse. The first document I have for this game is dated January of 2003.. And the latest one is dated June of 2004., so let me tell you how I remember this, and I’ll also tell you one thing that someone at least disagrees with me on. But so in 2003 we were contacted by interplay who told us that Bethesda was bidding on the Fallout IP. And did we want to bid? We did. This is where our memories diverge.

item.title: Troika Games' Lord of the Rings Demo (2001)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me Tim.

After talking about Arcanum 2 Journey to the Center of Arcanum yesterday, it reminded me that after we worked on the sequel and it got rejected, Troika actually worked on Lord of the Rings, so I’m going to show you that demo in just a second, but I thought I’d give you some context. So when the sequel idea got rejected, Sarah approached us with Lord of the Rings, they just gotten the rights to the book. Now the movies had come out, they didn’t have any movie rights, but they had rights to the book and they wanted to know if we had any ideas.
